title_en: Rhetorical Legacy: I'jaz al-Quran and Arabic Literary Criticism
title_ar: الإرث البلاغي: إعجاز القرآن والنقد الأدبي العربي
content_en:
Beyond the tafsir tradition itself, Al-Kashaf exerted a profound influence on the broader fields of Arabic literary criticism, rhetoric, and the study of the Quran's linguistic inimitability (i'jaz al-Quran). Al-Zamakhshari's analytical vocabulary became the standard language of Quranic rhetorical analysis, and his method of reading — attending to the grammar and rhetoric simultaneously, treating each as illuminating the other — shaped how subsequent generations understood Arabic eloquence at its highest expression.

The doctrine of i'jaz al-Quran holds that the Quran is inimitable — that no human being can produce a text equal to it in eloquence, wisdom, and impact, and that this inimitability is itself one of the proofs of the Quran's divine origin. Early scholars attempted to articulate what specifically made the Quran inimitable, and the answers they developed drew on the emerging disciplines of Arabic grammar and rhetoric. Al-Zamakhshari's contribution to this project was to demonstrate, verse by verse, that the Quran's linguistic choices were not arbitrary but precisely optimal — that each word, grammatical construction, and rhetorical device was exactly what the passage required for its meaning and effect.

His analysis of the Quran's use of 'iltifat' — the sudden shift in grammatical person (from third to second, or second to first) — is among his most celebrated contributions. He showed that these shifts, which might appear grammatically irregular to a reader expecting consistency, are in fact powerful rhetorical devices that draw the reader into the text, shift the emotional register, or emphasize a particular point. This insight became foundational in subsequent discussions of the Quran's style.

His analysis of Quranic imagery — the parables (amthal), the metaphors (isti'arah), and the extended comparisons — brought the tools of classical Arabic poetic criticism to bear on the Quran's prose. Al-Zamakhshari was deeply versed in Arabic poetry, and his commentary frequently draws on pre-Islamic and early Islamic poetry to illuminate the Arabic usage in specific Quranic phrases, helping readers understand the word's resonance in its linguistic environment.

Post-Al-Kashaf Arabic rhetoric (balagha) developed largely in response to the questions al-Zamakhshari raised. The systematic textbooks of balagha that became standard in the madrasa curriculum — works like al-Taftazani's Mukhtasar al-Ma'ani or al-Qazwini's Talkhis al-Miftah — organized and formalized the rhetorical categories that al-Zamakhshari applied in practice. In this sense, Al-Kashaf is not merely a commentary on the Quran but a foundational text in the history of Arabic literary theory.

For scholars working today on the literary dimension of the Quran, Al-Kashaf remains essential reading. Contemporary academic work on Quranic rhetoric, whether produced within the Islamic scholarly tradition or in Western academic settings, inevitably engages with al-Zamakhshari's analytical contributions. His influence on how educated readers perceive the Quran's language — as a text of extraordinary rhetorical precision and artistry — is one of his enduring legacies, quite apart from the theological debates his commentary also generated.
